question: What is Kotlin Multiplatform (KMP) and what does it share between iOS and Android?
answer:
KMP lets you write Kotlin once and target multiple platforms — Android, iOS, JVM/server, JS, Wasm, native desktop. It is NOT 'write UI once'; it shares non-UI code:

• Networking (Ktor client).
• Serialization (kotlinx.serialization).
• Persistence (SQLDelight, Realm KMP, Room KMP for Android+desktop).
• Domain layer / business rules.
• ViewModels (with `lifecycle-viewmodel-kmp`).
• Coroutines + Flow.

UI stays native (SwiftUI / UIKit on iOS, Compose / View on Android) — or you can also share UI with Compose Multiplatform (separate question).

Gain: one place for business logic, less drift between platforms, fewer bugs from divergence. Cost: tooling friction (Gradle, build), iOS interop quirks, smaller library ecosystem.

question: What are expect/actual declarations in KMP?
answer:
`expect`/`actual` is KMP's mechanism for platform-specific implementations of a shared API.

• In `commonMain` you write `expect class Foo` / `expect fun bar()` — declares the API with no body.
• In each platform source set (`androidMain`, `iosMain`, `jvmMain`) you provide `actual class Foo` / `actual fun bar()` with the platform implementation.
• The compiler ensures all expected declarations have an actual.

Use for things that genuinely need native APIs: file IO, secure storage, DateFormatter, Logger, UUID generation, BluetoothScanner. Don't expect/actual things that have a single common implementation.

Alternative pattern: define an interface in commonMain + provide platform implementations through DI; often cleaner than expect/actual.

question: How do you call KMP code from Swift? What are the interop pain points?
answer:
Kotlin/Native produces an Apple framework (`Shared.framework`) you import in Swift. Generally smooth, but with sharp edges:

• Suspend functions appear as async/throws (since Kotlin 1.4) — call from Swift's async/await.
• Flow is exposed as `Kotlinx_coroutines_coreFlow` — bridge with helpers (e.g. `KMP-NativeCoroutines`, `SKIE`) for AsyncSequence / Combine integration.
• Generics: Swift sees Kotlin generics, but with limited variance. Sealed classes appear as classes; you switch via `if let`/cast.
• Naming: package names are flattened; conflicts get resolved with `@ObjCName` annotations.
• Memory: Kotlin/Native uses ARC-like object freezing in old memory model (deprecated); the new memory model (default since 1.7) is closer to JVM and avoids the freezing pain.
• Tools: SKIE (Touchlab) generates Swift-friendly wrappers (sealed classes as enums, Flow as AsyncSequence) — strongly recommended.

Pain points: Kotlin lambda types appear as `(KotlinUnit) -> KotlinUnit`-style closures; Date / Decimal types map awkwardly; debugging requires source maps in both worlds.

question: What KMP libraries are typically used for networking, persistence, DI, and logging?
answer:
Mature KMP-ready libraries the community converges on:

• Networking — Ktor Client (engines per platform: OkHttp on Android, Darwin on iOS, JS, etc.). Handles JSON via kotlinx.serialization.
• Persistence — SQLDelight (typed SQL), Realm KMP, Room KMP (currently Android + JVM + iOS). Settings — `multiplatform-settings`.
• DI — Koin (multiplatform-friendly DSL) — the most common. Kodein KMP — alternative.
• Logging — Kermit (Touchlab) — multiplatform logging with platform-specific outputs.
• Date/time — `kotlinx-datetime`.
• Coroutines/Flow — `kotlinx-coroutines-core`.
• Crash/analytics — Sentry KMP, Firebase Crashlytics via KMP wrappers, RevenueCat KMP for IAP.
• Tools — Touchlab's SKIE (Swift code generation), KMP-NativeCoroutines (Flow/await bridges).

Key tip: pick libraries with active KMP support and same major version across platforms — older Android-only libs won't work.

question: How do you share ViewModels in KMP? What about navigation?
answer:
Sharing ViewModels:
• `lifecycle-viewmodel-kmp` (since 2024) — provides `ViewModel` in commonMain. State exposed as `StateFlow`. Android uses standard `viewModelScope`; iOS uses a manual scope you tear down on screen disappear (or via SKIE/MOKO MVVM helpers).
• MOKO MVVM, Decompose, Multiplatform-AppDi — older / alternative VM-and-state-management libraries.
• Roll-your-own — common-only ViewModel + bind it on each platform.

Sharing navigation:
• Decompose — full multiplatform navigation library with deep-link support, back-stack management, and lifecycle handling.
• Compose Multiplatform's Navigation (preview) — the official path forward; matches AndroidX Nav APIs.
• Or keep navigation native — SwiftUI NavigationStack on iOS, Jetpack Navigation on Android, with shared ViewModels driving state changes.

Pattern: shared ViewModel exposes `StateFlow<UiState>` and an `onAction(action: Action)` API. Native UI binds to it (Compose: `collectAsStateWithLifecycle`, SwiftUI: bridge Flow to AsyncSequence/Combine).

question: When is KMP a good fit and when is it not?
answer:
Good fits:
• Large business-logic-heavy app on both platforms (banking, e-commerce, content, ride-hailing).
• Long-lived product where divergence risk is real and expensive.
• Team comfortable with Gradle, Kotlin, and at least intermediate iOS work.
• Greenfield or progressively adoptable (start with one shared module).

Bad fits:
• Tiny apps (one product launch, < 6 months ahead). The setup cost dominates.
• Heavily UI-driven apps where shared logic is < 20% of the codebase.
• Teams without iOS expertise — KMP doesn't remove the need to ship a polished native iOS app.
• Shops standardised on React Native or Flutter for cost reasons — KMP doesn't share UI by default.

Trade-offs to budget:
• Build complexity (multi-target Gradle, framework packaging).
• Slightly slower iOS build.
• Tooling lag — some libraries Android-only.
• Onboarding cost for iOS engineers learning Kotlin.

KMP shines when shared business logic + native UI is the explicit goal. Don't bring it in just to write less code.
